full_text:
Single-cell Spatial Profiling of Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia Lesions

Naipunya Guruprasad
5th May 2026

Speaker notes:
Hi everyone, today I will be presenting the progress and results of the CIN2 project. 
 Please feel free to stop me at any point to ask me questions and most importantly to give feedback or suggestions for analysis.

BACKGROUND

Every year, hundreds of thousands of women are diagnosed with the various pre –cancerous stages of the cervix. 

Progression:

HPV infects the basal layer of epithelium in the squamocolumnar junction (SCJ)

The immune system usually clears the infection in 1-2 years

Speaker notes:
Every year, hundreds of thousands of women are diagnosed with the various pre –cancerous stages of the cervix. 

It doesn't appear suddenly; it develops through a stepwise process, starting from an HPV infection, progressing through precancerous lesions, and eventually into invasive cancer.

most Human Papilloma Virus infections are actually cleared by our immune system within one to two years. But HPV specifically targets the basal layer of the epithelium at the squamocolumnar junction, which is. a region in the cervix where two different cell types meet. This transition zone is particularly vulnerable because the cells there are actively remodelling, which makes it easier for the virus to infect and persist in.

BACKGROUND

Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia Grade 2 lesions (CIN2) represents an intermediate stage of moderate dysplasia where lesions either regress or progress to higher-grade disease.

Lizarazo-Taborda, M. d. R., Godínez-Rubí, M., Núnez-Avellaneda, D., Ramírez-de-Arellano, A., Pereira-Suárez, A. L., & Villegas-Pineda, J. C. (2025). Cervical Cancer Biomarkers in Non-Cervical Samples: Emerging Tools for Diagnosis and Prognosis. International Journal of Molecular Sciences, 26(13), 6502. https://doi.org/10.3390/ijms26136502

Speaker notes:
When the virus persists, it can lead to abnormal changes in the cervical cells called dysplasia. 
These changes are graded from CIN1 to CIN3 based on severity and CIN2 sits right in the middle, because it captures a mixture of CIN1, which are HPV-producing and relatively benign, and CIN3-like cells, which are more transformed and at higher risk of progression. And this is exactly what makes CIN2 such a critical and difficult stage to manage.

BACKGROUND

WHY & WHICH OF THESE LESIONS IN WOMEN REGRESS SPONTANEOUSLY WHILE OTHERS PROGRESS?

Speaker notes:
The current standard treatment for cervical pre-cancer is LEEP also known as Loop Electrosurgical Excision Procedure . In this procedure, a thin wire loop is used to remove the abnormal tissues from the cervix. It sounds straightforward, but it comes with real consequences like bleeding, infection, and importantly, complications in future pregnancies such as preterm birth.

What makes this even more problematic is that in a 2 year surveillance trial done right here at HUS, revealed that in 50 to 60 percent of CIN2 lesions in woomen actually regress on their own, without any treatment. That means the majority of women who undergo this procedure they may have never needed in the first place.

And that forms the core question driving this project: why and which CIN2 lesions regress spontaneously, while others progress — so that we can move towards a more personalised management and avoid unnecessary overtreatment.

Project overview

FPPE

Speaker notes:
To answer that question, we used formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded tissue samples collected from the HUS clinical trial. 

These tissue blocks were sectioned into adjacent slides, allowing the same tissue region to be analysed with three complementary methods: 

H&E for histology which was then used for a panoptic segmentation model to predict features of regression, T-CyCIF for single-cell sspatial profiling, and GeoMx for spatial transcriptomics.
Today I'll be focusing on the T-CyCIF arm of the study

AIMS

Identifying different cell phenotypes in the tissue microenvironment

Exploring Epithelial subclusters

Spatial analysis

To identify spatial biomarkers of CIN2 regression by systematically mapping the composition, spatial organisation, and interactions of immune cell populations within the lesion to facilitate clinical translation.

Speaker notes:
Our goal with T-CyCIF was to build on the H&E findings, which suggested that certain cell types and their spatial organisation influence whether a lesion regresses or progresses. To explore this at single-cell resolution, we structured the analysis in three steps: first, cell phenotyping to identify the major cell populations; second, subclustering of the epithelial compartment to uncover distinct epithelial states; and third, spatial analysis to understand how these cells are arranged and interact within the tissue.

THE COHORT

Created using

Speaker notes:
For the T-CyCIF analysis, we worked with 62 samples, grouped by clinical outcome. Quick-regressors are lesions that resolved within one year without treatment. Regressors resolved within two years. And non-regressors are lesions that had not regressed even after two years of follow-up

Tissue Cyclic Immunofluorescence (T-CyCIF)

https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing

22-plex antibody panel

Speaker notes:
Tissue Cyclic Immunofluorescence is a multiplexed imaging technique that uses repeated cycles of staining and imaging to capture dozens of protein markers simultaneously in a tissue.
We used a 22-plex antibody panel covering both lineage and functional markers. The scanned images were then processed through our in-house pipeline, which handles illumination correction, stitching with ASHLAR, cell segmentation with StarDist, quantification of protein expression, quality control with CyLinter and cell type calling using Tribus.

3,076,923 single cells segmented and phenotyped

CD3D

CD4

CD8

Lymphoid mask

IBA

Myeloid mask

Epithelial dysplastic mask

p16

DNA

Speaker notes:
So far, we’ve segmented and phenotyped around three million cells.

Speaker notes:
After phenotyping, we identified 11 major cell populations across 3 million cells. The UMAP shows that the cell types separate cleanly where stromal cells form a large distinct cloud at the top, the two epithelial populations sit together at the bottom reflecting their shared PanCK and p63 expression, and immune populations each occupy their own territory in the centre. 

The heatmap on the bottom validates the marker logic behind each assignment. for example, Epithelial_dysplastic cells are strongly p16 positive, and each immune subtype is defined by its expected lineage marker

Speaker notes:
This plot shows the cell type composition of every sample, along with the three clinical outcome annotations , HPV type, and E4 status on top. E4-positive samples reflect active viral replication and E4-negative samples suggest a more transformed state. Even with these annotations, it is difficult to see clear group-level differences by eye alone, which reflects the high sample-to-sample variability that is a hallmark of CIN2.

Leiden clustering: Epithelial subclusters

Clustering

DNA

p16

Ki-67

PanCK

p63

Speaker notes:
To go deeper into the epithelial compartment, i isolated all epithelial cells and applied Leiden clustering — an unsupervised graph-based algorithm that groups cells purely by their protein expression. We used nine markers covering proliferation, squamous differentiation, HPV activity, and immune interaction.

Leiden clustering: Epithelial subclusters

C0 : Basal (Normal Squamous-like)

C2 : Proliferating (Neoplastic-like)

C1 + C3 : Other-epithelial

Speaker notes:
Clustering revealed four distinct epithelial states.
Cluster 2 is the most transformed — it has the highest p16INK4a and Ki-67 expression, meaning these cells are actively proliferating. This is likely CIN3-like, neoplastic population.
Cluster 0 sits at the other end — moderate squamous markers, lower p16 — consistent with a basal, more normal-like state.

For C1 and C3, with a limited marker panel and no lineage confirmation, there is. no interpretation of them at this stage. though we can note that C1 shows elevated PD-L1 and pRb expression

DNA

p16

Ki-67

PanCK

p63

Epithelial normal

Epithelial dysplastic

Neoplastic-like

Other-epithelial

Squamous-like

Nuclei

T-CycIF

Masks

Clusters

Speaker notes:
We hoped that these data layers would give us insight into whether the epithelial state is driving regression

Other epithelial

Other epithelial

Speaker notes:
but when we compared the cluster cell type proportions across outcome groups, we did not find statistically significant differences. This told us that it is probably not purely driven by dysplasia in the epithelium.

Recurrent cell neighborhood : Spatial count vs LDA

Radii:
100µm 

k:
8

Grönholm, A. (2025). Profiling of spatial tumor microenvironment architecture in highly multiplexed images of high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (Doctoral dissertation, University of Helsinki). Helda – University of Helsinki. https://helda.helsinki.fi/items/e25ee9ec-c925-4a66-8893-e43cdd5aebc8

Speaker notes:
Next we wanted to look at the dfferent cellular neighbourhoods in our tissues so we tested two methods for characterising that. 

Spatial Count counts how many of each cell type fall within a defined radius around every cell, creating a neighbourhood composition. Spatial LDA on the other hand identifies recurring patterns of cell co-occurrence across the tissue.
 
After testing both methods across multiple radii, Spatial Count at 100µm with k=8 clusters — guided by an elbow curve — gave the most biologically interpretable result.

RCN analysis

Speaker notes:
This gave us 8 rcns. and the Shannon index on the right reflecting how diverse each neighbourhood is. 
RCN3, 5, and 6 are the most homogeneous — they are almosty purely dysplastic or stromal. At the other end, RCN8and 7 represents a, mixed immune environment. Other contain a mix of immune, stromal, and normal epithelial cells. 
When we looked at enrichment across outcome groups, RCN3 enriched in came up as the most enriched neighbourhood in regressing lesions.

RCN3 on image

Speaker notes:
But when we mapped this back onto the tissue, it became clear that RCN3 captures the interior of the dysplastic epithelium, not the boundary where we believe the key immune interactions are happening.

Investigating the LSI using SPACEstat -ongoing

Speaker notes:
so this pointed us toward a more targeted approach to quantify the region of lesion-stromal interface on the tissue.

To investigate LSI, I have just started using SPACEstat and will also try Pablo’s grid analysis. I got the first results this morning and haven't had time to fully inspect them, but I wanted to share some preliminary findings and get your input.

Generating the LSI boundary

Quick-regressor

Non-regressor

Speaker notes:
SPACEstat identified the LSI by finding cells within 50µm on both sides of the boundary on the dysplastic epithelium side and the stromal side. 

You can see this visualised here for samples from two of our outcome groups — the red cells mark the epithelial side of the interface and the blue cells mark the stromal side

Exploring immune cells in lesion proximity

Speaker notes:
Next i wanted to see which immune cells are in proximity to the lesion side of the interface, and i found that M2 macrophages and B cells had a nominally higher proportion within 50µm in quick-regressors compared to the other groups. However, these findings do not survive FDR correction, which is expected given our unequal sample sizes.

TAKEAWAYS

Highly heterogenous across outcome groups

Dysplasia enriched in non-regressing groups

Speaker notes:
So to conclude, our main takeaways from today is that CIN2 is highly heterogeneous across all outcome groups at the cellular level and that Dysplastic epithelium is enriched in non-regressors. 

And now some more preliminary spatial patterns so the next steps are to validate and inspect these immune cell populations at the interface.
